section.title: Titus and the Delegation
section.sectionRationale: Having appealed for the collection on the ground of grace and the example of Christ, Paul now attends to the practical integrity of the work. He is sending Titus and two trusted brothers ahead so that the large sum may be handled above reproach, and so that the Corinthians will be ready when he arrives. The passage shows the care Paul takes to guard his ministry from any hint of scandal and to secure a gift given willingly rather than grudgingly.
section.thesis: Because the handling of the churches' generosity must be honorable in the eyes of both God and men, Paul commissions a trusted delegation to oversee the collection and to prepare the Corinthians in advance, so that their gift may be ready as a genuine act of blessing and the confidence he has expressed in them may not prove empty.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the sending of Titus and the brothers whom God has stirred and the churches have chosen; the safeguard of doing what is right before God and men, with the messengers who are the glory of Christ; and the advance arrangements so the Corinthians will be ready and the boasting not be found empty.

units.label: Titus and the brothers are sent
units.note: the same devotion put into Titus's heart

verses.text: But thanks be to God, who put into the heart of Titus the same {{spoude|devotion}} I have for you.
For not only did he welcome our appeal, but he is eagerly coming to you of his own volition.
Along with Titus we are sending the brother who is praised by all the churches for his work in the gospel.
More than that, this brother was chosen by the churches to accompany us with the gracious offering we administer to honor the Lord Himself and to show our eagerness to help.

units.label: Doing what is right before God and men
units.note: the messengers, the glory of Christ

verses.text: We want to avoid any criticism of the way we administer this generous gift.
For we are taking great care to do what is right, not only in the eyes of the Lord, but also in the eyes of men.
And we are sending along with them our brother who has proven his earnestness to us many times and in many ways, and now even more so by his great confidence in you.
As for Titus, he is my partner and fellow worker among you. As for our brothers, they are messengers of the churches, the {{doxa|glory}} of Christ.
In full view of the churches, then, show these men the proof of your love and the reason for our boasting about you.

units.label: Ready, so the boast is not empty
units.note: a generous gift prepared, not begrudged

verses.text: Now about the service to the saints, there is no need for me to write to you.
For I know your eagerness to help, and I have been boasting to the Macedonians that since last year you in Achaia were prepared to give. And your zeal has stirred most of them to do likewise.
But I am sending the brothers in order that our boasting about you in this matter should not prove empty, but that you will be prepared, just as I said.
Otherwise, if any Macedonians come with me and find you unprepared, we, to say nothing of you, would be ashamed of having been so confident.

So I thought it necessary to urge the brothers to visit you beforehand and make arrangements for the generous gift you had promised. This way, your gift will be prepared generously and not begrudgingly.

spoude.gloss: Diligence, earnestness, eager devotion, haste in a good sense. Paul thanks God for putting into the heart of Titus the same spoude, the same earnest care, that Paul himself has for the Corinthians. The word describes a wholehearted zeal that does not drag its feet but presses eagerly toward the good work. It marks the whole delegation, who are commended repeatedly for their proven earnestness in the service of the gospel.

doxa.gloss: Glory, honor, radiance, the visible display of worth. Paul calls the brothers who carry the collection the glory of Christ, meaning that in their faithful service the honor and radiance of Christ are put on display. Their integrity and love reflect the character of their Lord, so that as they minister among the churches, it is Christ who is seen and magnified through them.

basic.context: Paul turns from the theology of giving to its careful administration. He is sending Titus, who shares Paul's own eager care for the Corinthians, along with two trusted brothers chosen by the churches, to complete the collection. He is determined that so large a sum be handled honorably before both God and men, and that the Corinthians be ready when he and possibly some Macedonians arrive, so the confidence he has voiced about them proves true.
basic.hermeneutics: This is not dull logistics but a window into ministry integrity. Paul's insistence on doing what is right in the eyes of men as well as the Lord teaches that transparent handling of money is a gospel matter. Read the delegation and the advance arrangements as a model of accountability that protects both the mission and the giver.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul thanks God for the spoude, the eager devotion, that God placed in Titus's heart, the same earnestness Paul has for the church. He calls the brothers who carry the gift the doxa, the glory, of Christ, so that in their faithful service the honor of Christ is displayed.
basic.typology: The brothers who carry the offering to Jerusalem, tested and trusted, stand in the line of faithful stewards throughout Scripture whose reliability reflects a greater Steward. Their transparent service anticipates the perfect faithfulness of Christ, who handled all that the Father gave Him without loss.
basic.christ: The brothers are called the glory of Christ, for in their integrity and love the character of the Lord shines out. Christ is the one whose own faithful stewardship of the Father's will they reflect, and it is His honor, not their own, that their labor is meant to display and magnify among the churches.
basic.god: It is God who put the eager devotion into Titus's heart, showing that even the willing servants of a good work are His gift. Paul's concern to do right before both God and men reflects a God who sees in secret yet also cares that His name be honored openly, so that the watching world sees the integrity of His people.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 12 urges zeal that is not slothful in serving the Lord. 1 Corinthians 16 arranges for approved men to carry the gift to Jerusalem. Proverbs 3 speaks of finding favor in the sight of God and man. 1 Peter 2 calls believers to keep their conduct honorable among the nations.
basic.oneStory: The gospel produces not only generous hearts but honest hands. As God's people share their resources across the world, He raises up faithful stewards whose integrity displays the glory of Christ, so that the ministry of giving is guarded from scandal and the name of the Lord is honored before a watching world.

context.literary: This section sits at the hinge of the collection appeal, between the theological grounding of 8:1 to 15 and the climactic teaching on cheerful giving in 9:6 to 15. It is largely practical, commending the members of the delegation one by one and explaining the advance visit. The repeated language of earnestness, proof, and boasting ties the arrangements to the sincerity of the Corinthians' love.
context.historical: Handling a large collection across the ancient Mediterranean was fraught with risk of theft and, worse for Paul, of the suspicion that he might profit from it. By sending a delegation chosen by the churches themselves, Paul built in accountability and shielded his integrity. The mention of possible Macedonian companions reflects the real travel plans behind the letter and the social weight of shame and honor in that culture.
context.canonical: The care Paul takes here matches his charge in 1 Corinthians 16 that the Corinthians choose their own approved men to carry the gift. It reflects a consistent New Testament concern that ministry, and especially money in ministry, be handled transparently, a principle echoed in the qualifications for church leaders who must not be lovers of money and must be above reproach.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To secure the completion of the collection through trustworthy messengers, to protect his own integrity and the reputation of the ministry by transparent handling of the funds, and to prompt the Corinthians to be fully ready so that his confidence in them is vindicated.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific arrangements, Titus and the two brothers, the advance visit, are descriptive of Paul's situation. Yet they embody prescriptive principles: that money in ministry should be handled with accountability, that leaders should aim at what is honorable before both God and men, and that promises of generosity should be followed through.

debates.question: Who are the two unnamed brothers sent with Titus?

views.label: Deliberately unnamed representatives
views.case: Paul leaves them anonymous, identifying them by their reputation and their appointment by the churches rather than by name, perhaps because their names were carried verbally by Titus; the point is their proven character, not their identity.

views.label: Identifiable co-workers such as Luke or others
views.case: Some have proposed candidates like Luke for the brother praised in the gospel, or figures such as Trophimus or Tychicus, drawing on Acts and Paul's travel companions, though the text itself gives no names.

debates.bearing: Their anonymity does not weaken the passage; whether or not we can name them, Paul's stress falls on their tested integrity and churchly appointment, which is what makes them fit to handle the gift.

debates.question: Why does Paul emphasize doing right in the eyes of men as well as of the Lord?

views.label: Guarding the gospel's reputation
views.case: Paul aims to prevent any slander that could discredit his ministry and the collection, since visible integrity protects the credibility of the gospel before a watching world.

views.label: Protecting the consciences of givers
views.case: Transparent handling reassures the churches that their gifts are used faithfully, sustaining trust and encouraging continued generosity within the body.

debates.bearing: Both concerns hold together; Paul cares for God's approval first, yet knows that visible honesty before men both shields the gospel and nurtures the trust on which generous fellowship depends.

study.originalLanguages: Paul opens with thanks to God who put into the heart of Titus the same spoude, the eager devotion or earnest zeal, that Paul himself feels for the Corinthians; the word runs through the passage as the mark of the whole delegation, tested and proven in earnestness. At the climax he calls the brothers the doxa, the glory, of Christ, for their faithful service puts the honor and radiance of the Lord on display among the churches. Between these two words lies Paul's insistence on what is honorable before both God and men, so that the language of devotion and glory is grounded in the concrete integrity of handling the gift without reproach.

typology.type: The tested and trusted stewards of the offering
typology.fulfillment: Their proven faithfulness in carrying the churches' gift reflects the pattern of the faithful steward throughout Scripture and points to Christ, who handled all the Father entrusted to Him and lost none.

typology.type: Messengers who are the glory of Christ
typology.fulfillment: Servants in whom the honor of the Lord is displayed anticipate the day when Christ is glorified in all His people, and their reflected glory finds its source in Him.

study.christ: Christ is named directly when Paul calls the brothers the glory of Christ, meaning that in their integrity and love the Lord Himself is seen. The whole passage serves the collection that expresses the unity of Christ's body and the compassion He works in His people. In their tested faithfulness the messengers mirror the perfect faithfulness of Christ, the true and faithful servant who stewarded the Father's will without failure, and it is His honor their labor is meant to magnify.
study.god: God is the first actor in the passage: it is He who put the eager devotion into the heart of Titus, so that even the willing servants of the collection are His gift to the church. Paul's determination to do what is right in the eyes of the Lord shows a God who searches hearts and to whom all ministry is finally accountable. Yet God is also honored when His people are seen to act honestly before men, so that His name is not slandered but adorned by the integrity of those who serve Him.

crossReferences.note: Paul arranges for approved men, accredited by letter, to carry the gift to Jerusalem, matching the delegation here.
Keep loyalty and faithfulness and you will find favor in the sight of God and man, echoed in Paul's aim.
Do not be slothful in zeal, be fervent in spirit, serving the Lord, the spoude Paul commends in Titus.
Keep your conduct honorable among the nations, so that they may see your good deeds and glorify God.

study.oneStory: The story of redemption creates a worldwide family that shares its resources across every barrier. To carry that generosity faithfully, God raises up trustworthy servants whose integrity puts the glory of Christ on display. This passage shows the gospel producing not only open hearts but honest hands, guarding the ministry of giving from scandal so that the Lord is honored both in heaven and before a watching world.

quiz.q: Why does Paul give thanks to God concerning Titus?

options.text: Because God put into Titus's heart the same earnest devotion Paul had for the Corinthians.
options.note: Paul credits God for the eager care he found in Titus.

options.text: Because Titus had volunteered to raise the most money.
options.note: The emphasis is on God-given devotion, not fundraising totals.

options.text: Because Titus needed to be persuaded to go.
options.note: Titus came eagerly of his own volition, welcoming the appeal.

options.text: Because Titus had rebuked the Corinthians severely.
options.note: The passage speaks of devotion and eagerness, not rebuke.

quiz.q: Why does Paul take such care in administering the collection?

options.text: To do what is right in the eyes of the Lord and also in the eyes of men, avoiding any criticism.
Because he did not trust the Corinthians at all.
options.note: The concern is integrity in handling funds, not distrust of the givers.

options.text: Because the law required a certain procedure.
options.note: It is gospel integrity, not a legal procedure, that drives him.

options.text: Because he wanted a share of the money for himself.
options.note: Paul guards precisely against any such suspicion.

quiz.q: What does Paul call the brothers sent with the offering?

options.text: Messengers of the churches, the glory of Christ.
options.note: Verse 23 names them the glory of Christ, displaying His honor.

options.text: Officials appointed by the Roman authorities.
options.note: They were chosen by the churches, not by civil authorities.

options.text: Untested newcomers taking their first assignment.
options.note: They were proven and praised, not untested.

options.text: Paid agents hired to transport funds.
options.note: They are servants of the churches reflecting Christ, not hired agents.

quiz.q: Why does Paul send the brothers ahead before he arrives?

options.text: So the Corinthians would be ready and his boasting about them would not prove empty.
options.note: Verses 3 to 5 give exactly this reason.

options.text: So they could collect the gift by force if needed.
options.note: The gift was to be given willingly, not extracted by force.

options.text: Because he no longer intended to visit himself.
options.note: Paul plans to come, possibly with Macedonians, and wants them ready.

options.text: So the money could be spent before he came.
options.note: The aim was to prepare the gift generously, not to spend it early.

questions: Paul thanks God for the devotion He placed in Titus's heart. How does it change your view of Christian service to see even the willingness to serve as a gift from God?
Paul insisted on doing what is right in the eyes of men as well as the Lord. Where in your life or work does your integrity need to be visible and not merely private?
The brothers are called the glory of Christ because His honor shone through their service. Whose faithful service has shown you something of Christ, and how might yours do the same?
Paul arranged accountability so no one could criticize how the money was handled. How seriously do you take transparency in the way money is handled in the church?
Paul wanted the gift prepared willingly and not begrudgingly. What would it take for your giving to be ready and glad rather than last-minute and reluctant?

translationNotes.note: The gracious offering that we administer refers to the Jerusalem collection; a source footnote points to 1 Corinthians 16:3 to 4 for the parallel arrangements.
Messengers of the churches renders a word that a footnote notes may also be translated apostles, here in the wider sense of sent representatives rather than the twelve apostles.
The original sets off the phrase to say nothing of you with dashes; it is rendered here with commas for a clean reading without altering the sense.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 8:16-24 and 9:1-5. Em dashes in 9:4 were replaced with commas for a clean reading; no other dash substitutions were required. Footnote markers were removed. Sermon set to none; no expositor URL was verified this session.
